subject: Physics
q:
A plane is inclined at an angle α=30° with respect to the horizontal. A particle is projected with a speed  u=2 m s-1 , from the base of the plane, making an angle θ=15° with respect to the plane as shown in the figure. The distance from the base, at which the particle hits the plane is close to:
(Take  g=10 m s-2 )
question:
A plane is inclined at an angle α=30° with respect to the horizontal. A particle is projected with a speed  u=2 m s-1 , from the base of the plane, making an angle θ=15° with respect to the plane as shown in the figure. The distance from the base, at which the particle hits the plane is close to:
(Take  g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Range =2u2sinαcos⁡α+θgcos2⁡θ
α=angle of inclination=30°
θ=angle of projection from inclination=15°
u=2 m/s
R=222sin15°cos⁡15°+30°gcos2⁡30°=8sin⁡15°cos45°g cos230°  sin15°=3-122
R=453-1 3≈0.2 m
R≈20 cm

subject: Physics
q: The initial speed of a projectile fired from ground is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math>. At the highest point during its motion, the speed of projectile is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>u</mi></math>. The time of flight of the projectile is:
question: The initial speed of a projectile fired from ground is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math>. At the highest point during its motion, the speed of projectile is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>u</mi></math>. The time of flight of the projectile is:
solution:
At maximum height, only horizontal velocity of the particle remains. Let the angle of project be θ. The horizontal velocity of the particle will remain same, therefore

ucosθ=3u2⇒θ=30°

Now, time of flight of projectile is

T=2usinθg=ug

subject: Physics
q: Two objects are projected with same velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> however at different angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with the horizontal. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the ratio of horizontal range of the first object to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> object will be :
question: Two objects are projected with same velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> however at different angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> with the horizontal. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the ratio of horizontal range of the first object to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mi>nd</mi></msup></math> object will be :
solution:
Given: α+β=90°

Range of the first projectile:

R1=u2sin2αg

Range of the second projectile:

R2=u2sin2βg=u2sin(180°-2α)g=u2sin2αg

As we can see, both are equal.

R1=R2

Therefore,

R1:R2=1:1

subject: Physics
q: The position vector of a moving body at any instant of time is given as $\vec{r}=\left(5 t^2 \hat{i}-5 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{m}$. The magnitude and direction of velocity at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is,
question: The position vector of a moving body at any instant of time is given as $\vec{r}=\left(5 t^2 \hat{i}-5 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{m}$. The magnitude and direction of velocity at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is,
options: $5 \sqrt{15} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, making an angle of $\tan ^{-1} 4$ with - ve $Y$ axis
$5 \sqrt{15} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, making an angle of $\tan ^{-1} 4$ with + ve X axis
$5 \sqrt{17} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, making an angle of $\tan ^{-1} 4$ with + ve $X$ axis
$5 \sqrt{17} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, making an angle of $\tan ^{-1} 4$ with - ve $Y$ axis
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=5 t^2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=10 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=20 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \quad \text { at } \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \tan \theta=\frac{20}{5}=4 \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1} 4 \\ & \text { From -veY-axis }\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A ball is projected from the ground with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with horizontal so that its range and maximum height are equal, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> will be equal to
question: A ball is projected from the ground with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> with horizontal so that its range and maximum height are equal, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> will be equal to
solution:
The range and the maximum height of the projectile are given as below,

R=u2sin2θg=2u2sinθcosθgH=u2sin2θ2g

Therefore,

As H=R

⇒tanθ=4

subject: Physics
q: The water is filled up to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in a tank having vertical sidewalls. A hole is made in one of the walls at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> below the water level. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> for which the emerging stream of water strikes the ground at the maximum range is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The water is filled up to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in a tank having vertical sidewalls. A hole is made in one of the walls at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> below the water level. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> for which the emerging stream of water strikes the ground at the maximum range is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
R=2gh×(12-h)×2 g

4 h(12-h)=R

For maximum R

dRdh=0

⇒h=6 m

subject: Physics
q: A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass moves with a uniform speed in a horizontal circular groove, with vertical side walls of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the block takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to complete one round, the normal force by the side walls of the groove is:
question: A block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass moves with a uniform speed in a horizontal circular groove, with vertical side walls of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the block takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to complete one round, the normal force by the side walls of the groove is:
solution:
N=mω2R

Given m=0.2 kg,T=40 S,R=0.2 m

Put values in equation 1

N=9.859×10-4 N

subject: Physics
q: The trajectory of projectile, projected from the ground is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>20</mn></mfrac></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> are measured in meter. The maximum height attained by the projectile will be.
question: The trajectory of projectile, projected from the ground is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>20</mn></mfrac></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> are measured in meter. The maximum height attained by the projectile will be.
solution:
The trajectory of a projectile is parabolic in nature. At maximum height, it can be written that

 dydx=0   ...1

Substitute the expression for trajectory into equation (1) and solve to calculate the horizontal distance, for which the projectile attains the maximum height.
ddxx-x220=0⇒1-x10=0⇒x=10 m

Substitute the value of x in the given expression for the trajectory to obtain the maximum height.

y=10-10220 m= 5 m

subject: Physics
q: A water fountain on the ground sprinkles water all around it. If the speed of water coming out of the fountain is $\mathrm{v}$, the total area around the fountain that gets wet is :
question: A water fountain on the ground sprinkles water all around it. If the speed of water coming out of the fountain is $\mathrm{v}$, the total area around the fountain that gets wet is :
solution:
Max. range $=\frac{u^2}{\mathrm{~g}}$ i.e., $\frac{v^2}{\mathrm{~g}}$ (radius of circle)
$$
\text { Area occupied }=\pi\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{~g}}\right)^2=\frac{\pi \mathrm{v}^4}{\mathrm{~g}^2}
$$

subject: Physics
q: In a car race on straight road, car A takes a time t less than car B at the finish and passes finishing point with a speed v more than that of car B. Both the cars start from rest and travel with constant acceleration a1 and a2  respectively. Then v is equal to:
question: In a car race on straight road, car A takes a time t less than car B at the finish and passes finishing point with a speed v more than that of car B. Both the cars start from rest and travel with constant acceleration a1 and a2  respectively. Then v is equal to:
solution:
Given, 
Initially both car is at rest, so u1=u2=0
Acceleration of car A & B is a1 & a2

Let us assume,
Time of reach to destination of car A is, t1=t0
Time of reach to destination of car B is, t2=t0+t

Using second equation of motion, we have
u1t+12a1t12=u2t+12a2t22
⇒0×t+12a1t02=0×t+12a2t0+t2
⇒a1a2t0=t0+t
⇒t0=ta1a2-1

From first equation of motion, we have
v1=a1t0 &v2=a2t0+t
⇒v=v1-v2=a1-a2t0-a2t
⇒v=a1-a2ta1a2-1-a2t
⇒v=ta1a2a1-a2-a2a2a1-a2-a2
⇒v=ta1a2-a2a1a1-a2
⇒v=a1.a2 t

subject: Physics
q: A river is flowing from west to east direction with speed of $9 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$. If a boat capable of moving at a maximum speed of $27 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ in still water, crosses the river in half a minute, while moving with maximum speed at an angle of $150^{\circ}$ to direction of river flow, then the width of the river is :
question: A river is flowing from west to east direction with speed of $9 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$. If a boat capable of moving at a maximum speed of $27 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ in still water, crosses the river in half a minute, while moving with maximum speed at an angle of $150^{\circ}$ to direction of river flow, then the width of the river is :
solution:
$\therefore \mathrm{V}_{\perp}=$ river flow $=27 \times \cos 60^{\circ}=\frac{27}{2} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.
Time taken $=30 \mathrm{sec}$.
$\therefore \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{Vt}=\frac{27}{2} \times \frac{5}{18} \times 30 \mathrm{~m}=112.5 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves such that its position vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow></mrow><mi>t</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow></mrow><mi>t</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> where <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is a constant and <math><mi>t</mi></math> is time. Then which of the following statements is true for the velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the particle:
question: A particle moves such that its position vector <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow></mrow><mi>t</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow></mrow><mi>t</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> where <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is a constant and <math><mi>t</mi></math> is time. Then which of the following statements is true for the velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and acceleration <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of the particle:
options: <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is perpendicular to <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is directed away from the origin

<math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is perpendicular to <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> is directed towards the origin
solution:
r→=cos⁡ωti^+sin⁡ωtj^
v→=dr→dt=ω-sin⁡ωti^+cos⁡ωtj^
a→=dv→dt=-ω2cos⁡ωti^+sin⁡ωtj^
a→=-ω2r→∴a→ is antiparallel to r→
v→.r→=ω-sin⁡ωtcos⁡ωt+cos⁡ωtsin⁡ωt=0
So v→⊥r→

subject: Physics
q: A passenger train of length <math><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. Another freight train of length <math><mn>120</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. The ratio of times taken by the passenger train to completely cross the freight train when: (i) they are moving in the same direction, and (ii) in the opposite directions is:
question: A passenger train of length <math><mn>60</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. Another freight train of length <math><mn>120</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> travels at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>. The ratio of times taken by the passenger train to completely cross the freight train when: (i) they are moving in the same direction, and (ii) in the opposite directions is:
solution:
t1=260+12080-30

t2=260+12080+30

t1t2=115

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving with a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a circular horizontal track of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A bob is suspended from the roof of the car by a massless string. The angle made by the string with the vertical will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A car is moving with a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a circular horizontal track of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A bob is suspended from the roof of the car by a massless string. The angle made by the string with the vertical will be : (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let the frame of reference to be attached to the car with x-axis along horizontal direction and y-axis along vertical direction. Since the frame of reference is a rotating frame, a centrifugal force term should be added while applying Newton's second law.

Let θ be the angle made by the pendulum with the vertical.

Applying Newton's second law for horizontal direction:

Tsinθ -mv2R=0  (since the acceleration of the pendulum in the rotating frame is 0).

∴Tsinθ =mv2R    ..........(1)

Applying Newton's second law for vertical direction:

Tcosθ-mg=0

⇒Tcosθ=mg   ..........(2)

Dividing equation 1 by equation 2:

tanθ=v2Rg=2024010=1⇒θ=π4

subject: Physics
q: The range of the projectile projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>15</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the projectile is projected with same velocity at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> with horizontal, then its range will be
question: The range of the projectile projected at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>15</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If the projectile is projected with same velocity at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup></math> with horizontal, then its range will be
solution:
The data given is 

θ1=15°θ2=45°R1=50 m

The formula for range of a projectile is given by 

R=u2 sin2θg   ...(i)

Substituting the values in equation (i)

R1=u2sin2θ1g⇒50=u2sin30°g⇒u2g=100

Let the new range be R2. The magnitude of the range is 

R2=u2sin2θ2g⇒R2=u2gsin90°⇒R2=100 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
question: A particle is moving along a circular path with a constant speed of $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What is the magnitude of the change in velocity of the particle, when it moves through an angle of $60^{\circ}$ around the centre of the circle?
solution:
Change in velocity,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
|\Delta \overline{\mathrm{v}}|=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{v}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{2} \cos (\pi-\theta)} \\
=2 \mathrm{v} \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \quad\left(\because\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{1}\right|=\left|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{2}\right|\right)=\mathrm{v} \\
=(2 \times 10) \times \sin \left(30^{\circ}\right)=2 \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \\
=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{array}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
question: A small block slides down from the top of hemisphere of radius R=3 m as shown in the figure. The height h at which the block will lose contact with the surface of the sphere is m. (Assume there is no friction between the block and the hemisphere)
solution:
mgcosθ=mv2R   ...(1)

cosθ=hR

Energy conservation

mg{R-h}=12mv2  ...(2)

 from (1) and (2) ⇒mghR=2mg{R-h}R

h=2R3=2 m

subject: Physics
q:
A boy ties a stone of mass 100 g to the end of a 2 m long string and whirls it around in a horizontal plane. The string can withstand the maximum tension of 80 N. If the maximum speed with which the stone can revolve is Kπ rev min-1. The value of K is :
(Assume the string is massless and un-stretchable)
question:
A boy ties a stone of mass 100 g to the end of a 2 m long string and whirls it around in a horizontal plane. The string can withstand the maximum tension of 80 N. If the maximum speed with which the stone can revolve is Kπ rev min-1. The value of K is :
(Assume the string is massless and un-stretchable)
solution:
The maximum angular velocity of the stone is ω=rpm×2π60=K30 rad s-1

The tension in the string will provide the required centripetal force. Therefore,

T=mω2l⇒80=0.1×K302×2⇒K2=360000⇒K=600

subject: Physics
q: A bead of mass m stays at point Pa, b on a wire bent in the shape of a parabola y=4Cx2 and rotating with angular speed ω (see figure). The value of ω is (neglect friction)
question: A bead of mass m stays at point Pa, b on a wire bent in the shape of a parabola y=4Cx2 and rotating with angular speed ω (see figure). The value of ω is (neglect friction)
solution:
mω2acosθ=mgsinθ

ω=gtanθa

y=4cx2

tanθ=dydx=8xC

(tanθ)a,b=8aC

ω=g×8aca=22gC

subject: Physics
q: Two stones are projected from the top of a cliff $\mathrm{h}$ metres high, with the same speed $\mathrm{u}$, so as to hit the ground at the same spot. If one of the stones is projected at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal then the $\theta$ equals
question: Two stones are projected from the top of a cliff $\mathrm{h}$ metres high, with the same speed $\mathrm{u}$, so as to hit the ground at the same spot. If one of the stones is projected at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal then the $\theta$ equals
solution:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{u} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}}}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) \times \mathrm{t}$
$t=\frac{1}{\cos \theta} \sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}$

subject: Physics
q:
A body of mass 10 kg is projected at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The trajectory of the body is observed to pass through a point 20,10. If T is the time of flight, then its momentum vector, at time t=T2, is _____ .
[Take g=10 m s-2]
question:
A body of mass 10 kg is projected at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The trajectory of the body is observed to pass through a point 20,10. If T is the time of flight, then its momentum vector, at time t=T2, is _____ .
[Take g=10 m s-2]
solution:
It is given that the projectile passes through 20,10 and angle of projection is θ=45°. Using the trajectory equation,

y=xtanθ-gx22u2cos2θ

⇒10=20-10100u2

⇒u=20 m s-1

Now the time of flight of projectile,

T=2usinθg

⇒T=220210=22 s⇒T2=2 s

Now velocity of the projectile at any time t can be written as,

v→=ucosθi^+usinθ-gtj^

⇒v→=102i^+(102-102]j^ m s-1

Therefore, momentum p→=Mv→=1002i^+1002-200j^ N s

subject: Physics
q: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
question: Two balls with same mass and initial velocity, are projected at different angles in such a way that maximum height reached by first ball is 8 times higher than that of the second ball. $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the total flying times of first and second ball, respectively, then the ratio of $T_1$ and $T_2$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1=8 \times\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_1}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=8 \times \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta_2}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta_1=2 \sqrt{2} \sin \theta_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_1 / \mathrm{g}}{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta_2 / \mathrm{g}}=\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}=2 \sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: When a body is projected at an angle 45°, its range is maximum.

Reason R: For maximum range, the value of sin 2θ should be equal to one.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A: When a body is projected at an angle 45°, its range is maximum.

Reason R: For maximum range, the value of sin 2θ should be equal to one.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
Horizontal range of a projectile from a horizontal plane is given by, R=u2sin2θg.

Clearly for maximum range, sin2θ=1.

Therefore, θ=45°.

If she starts running without umbrella with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the rain drops hit her head vertically. The speed of rain drops with respect to the moving girl is
question: A girl standing on road holds her umbrella at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the vertical to keep the rain away. If she starts running without umbrella with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the rain drops hit her head vertically. The speed of rain drops with respect to the moving girl is
solution:
v→rg=v→r-v→g

 

From the above diagram, velocity of the raindrop wrt ground will be,

v→r=1522+1522=30 km h-1

And velocity of the raindrop wrt the moving girl will be,

v→rg=152=302 km h-1

subject: Physics
q: A boy reaches the airport and finds that the escalator is not working. He walks up the stationary escalator in time t1. If he remains stationary on a moving escalator then the escalator takes him up in time t2. The time taken by him to walk up on the moving escalator will be:
question: A boy reaches the airport and finds that the escalator is not working. He walks up the stationary escalator in time t1. If he remains stationary on a moving escalator then the escalator takes him up in time t2. The time taken by him to walk up on the moving escalator will be:
solution:
L= Length of escalator

Vb/ecc=Lt1

When only escalator is moving, 

Vesc=Lt2

when both are moving, 

Vb/g=Vb/esc+Vesc

Vb/g=Lt1+Lt2⇒t=LVb/g=t1t2t1+t2

subject: Physics
q: A spring mass system (mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> spring constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math><mi>l</mi></math> ) rests in equilibrium on a horizontal disc. The free end of the spring is fixed at the centre of the disc. If the disc together with spring mass system rotates about it's axis with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> the relative change in the length of the spring is best given by the option:
question: A spring mass system (mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> spring constant <math><mi>k</mi></math> and natural length <math><mi>l</mi></math> ) rests in equilibrium on a horizontal disc. The free end of the spring is fixed at the centre of the disc. If the disc together with spring mass system rotates about it's axis with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> the relative change in the length of the spring is best given by the option:
solution:
mω2l0+x=kx
l0x+1=kmω2
x=l0mω2k-mω2
k≫mω2
So, xl0 is equal to mω2k

subject: Physics
q: Two projectile thrown at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively, reach the maximum height in same time. The ratio of their initial velocities is
question: Two projectile thrown at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively, reach the maximum height in same time. The ratio of their initial velocities is
solution:
Time taken to reach maximum height is given by 

t=usinθg, where, u is initial velocity.

Here, t1=t2

∴u1sinθ1g=u2sinθ2g

⇒u1sin30=u2sin45

⇒u1u2=1212=21=2:1

The speed of the flow is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.

The speed of the flow is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.
solution:
10 sin 30°=x
x=5 m/s

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected with velocity $u$ so that its horizontal range is three times the maximum height attained by it. The horizontal range of the projectile is given as $\frac{n u^2}{25 g}$, where value of $n$ is : (Given ' $g$ ' is the acceleration due to gravity).
question: A particle is projected with velocity $u$ so that its horizontal range is three times the maximum height attained by it. The horizontal range of the projectile is given as $\frac{n u^2}{25 g}$, where value of $n$ is : (Given ' $g$ ' is the acceleration due to gravity).
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Range }=3 \mathrm{H}_{\max } \\ & \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{~g}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}}\end{aligned}$
$2 \sin \theta \cos \theta=\frac{3}{2} \sin ^2 \theta$
$\tan \theta=\frac{4}{3} \Rightarrow \theta=53^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2\left(2 \times \frac{3}{5} \times \frac{4}{5}\right)}{\mathrm{g}} \Rightarrow \frac{24 \mathrm{u}^2}{25 \mathrm{~g}}$

subject: Physics
q: If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the times of flight of two particles having the same initial velocity $u$ and range $\mathrm{R}$ on the horizontal, then $\mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2$ is equal to
question: If $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the times of flight of two particles having the same initial velocity $u$ and range $\mathrm{R}$ on the horizontal, then $\mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2$ is equal to
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_1=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \alpha}{\mathrm{g}}, \mathrm{t}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \beta}{\mathrm{g}}$ where $\alpha+\beta=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \mathrm{t}_1^2+\mathrm{t}_2^2=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}^2}{\mathrm{~g}^2}$

subject: Physics
q:
If the initial velocity in horizontal direction of a projectile is unit vector i^ and the equation of trajectory is y=5x1-x. The y component vector of the initial velocity is _____  j^

(Take g=10 m s-2)
question:
If the initial velocity in horizontal direction of a projectile is unit vector i^ and the equation of trajectory is y=5x1-x. The y component vector of the initial velocity is _____  j^

(Take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Given here, initial velocity, ux=1

Equation of trajectory, y=5 x1-x=5x-5x2

Now, differentiating the above equation, 

dydt=5dxdt-10xdxdt

For initial y-component of velocity

uy=dydtx=0⇒51=5

u→y=5j^

subject: Physics
q: A clock has a continuously moving second's hand of 0.1m length. The average acceleration of the tip of the hand (in units of ms-2 ]) is of the order of :
question: A clock has a continuously moving second's hand of 0.1m length. The average acceleration of the tip of the hand (in units of ms-2 ]) is of the order of :
solution:
The instantaneous acceleration of tip is given by

ac=ω2R=2π602×0.1=1900≈10-3

q: A player kicks a football with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the ground. What are the maximum height and the time taken by the football to reach at the highest point during motion? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A player kicks a football with an initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the ground. What are the maximum height and the time taken by the football to reach at the highest point during motion? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
θ=45°

H=u2sin2θ2g=(25)2×122×10=1258 m

and time t=T2

=usinθg=251210=25102=522s

subject: Physics
q: A child of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is going round a merry-go-round that makes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> rotation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The radius of the merry-go-round is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The centrifugal force on the child will be
question: A child of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is going round a merry-go-round that makes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> rotation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The radius of the merry-go-round is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The centrifugal force on the child will be
solution:
The angular speed ω of the child can be calculated as follows:

ω=2π3.14=2 rad s-1

The formula to calculate the centrifugal force on the child is given by

Fr=mω2r   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required centrifugal force.

Fr=5 kg×2 rad s-12×2 m=40 N
